content.text:

https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/30940/
https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/31021/
https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/30682/
https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/31016/

https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/30990/
https://www.eso.org/public/images/archive/category/360pano/
https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/31007/
I'll fetch each of these SVS pages to see what they are and what image resolutions are available.
I'll fetch all nine pages in parallel, and check how backdrops are currently stored in the repo.

# Spiral Galaxy M106 - Visualization Details

## Title & Subject
**Title:** "Spiral Galaxy M106"

**Subject:** A composite image of the nearby spiral galaxy M106 focusing on its "active center, where large amounts of gas are thought to be falling into and fueling a supermassive black hole."

---

## Downloadable Files

### Images
1. **REDACTED.jpg**
   - Dimensions: 1024×799 pixels

   - Size: 75.9 MB

3. **REDACTED.png**

## Credit Line

Web search results for query: "esahubble.org Pillars of Creation Eagle Nebula M16 2015 full resolution download original"

Links: [{"title":"Hubble Spies Cosmic Pillar in Eagle Nebula - NASA Science","url":"https://science.nasa.gov/missions/hubble/hubble-spies-cosmic-pillar-in-eagle-nebula/"},{"title":"Pillars of Creation Star in New Visualization from NASA's ...","url":"https://esahubble.org/videos/heic1501f/"},{"title":"New view of the Pillars of Creation — visible | ESA/Hubble","url":"https://esahubble.org/images/heic1501a/"},{"title":"Tour of Eagle Nebula | ESA/Hubble","url":"https://esahubble.org/videos/heic1501e/"},{"title":"New view of the Pillars of Creation — infrared | ESA/Hubble","url":"https://esahubble.org/images/heic1501b/"},{"title":"Hubblecast 82: New view of the Pillars of Creation | ESA/Hubble","url":"https://esahubble.org/videos/heic1501a/"},{"title":"Pillars of Creation | ESA/Hubble","url":"https://esahubble.org/images/opo9544a/"},{"title":"1 min read","url":"https://science.nasa.gov/?p=466434"},{"title":"Suggested Searches","url":"https://science.nasa.gov/asset/hubble/eagle-nebula-pillars-of-creation"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about the 2015 Hubble Space Telescope images of the Pillars of Creation in the Eagle Nebula (M16):

The NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope has revisited one of its most iconic and popular images: the Eagle Nebula's Pillars of Creation. Hubble released two images: a new visible-light image and an infrared light image of the region.

The observations were made in late 2014 with the Wide Field Camera 3 and released on January 5, 2015, to kick off Hubble's 25th year in orbit. With its higher resolution, the new camera provides a sharper view of the pillars and also presents a wider vista, showing the base of the pillars and more of the region surrounding them.

The ESA/Hubble website (esahubble.org) hosts these images at:
- https://esahubble.org/images/heic1501a/ (visible light image)
- https://esahubble.org/images/heic1501b/ (infrared image)

You can download various resolution versions of these images directly from the ESA/Hubble website or from NASA's official asset pages.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The listing is embedded as JS — let me parse the full ESO 360 panorama catalogue and filter to true 2:1 equirectangular images.

input.command:
cd /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-rashford-Developer-Float/e2618adc-48c8-4631-880a-a25b0a36608e/scratchpad && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
h=open('eso_viewall.html').read()
i=h.find('var images = [')
blk=h[i:h.find('];',i)]
ents=re.findall(r"id: '([^']*)',\s*title: '(.*?)',\s*width: (\d+),\s*height: (\d+)", blk, re.S)
print("total entries:", len(ents))
eq=[e for e in ents if abs(int(e[2])/int(e[3]) - 2.0) < 0.02]
print("2:1 equirectangular:", len(eq))
for id,t,w,hh in sorted(eq, key=lambda e:-int(e[2])):
    print(f"{int(w):>6} x {int(hh):<6}  {id:<45} {t}")
EOF

input.command: grep -nE "galax|nebula texture|distant|billboard|feature|panorama|equirect|backdrop" starfield_immersive_spec.md | head -40
input.description: Find spec sections on distant objects and backdrops

result.output:
12:- Feasible for a solo experienced dev. **Genuinely-3D animated prototype: ~1.5–2 weeks. Full polished build with every feature below: ~6.5–9 weeks solo** (see §12 for the breakdown). The core "float in a 3D starfield" is a few days; the range is driven by the interaction, physics, and content features layered on top.
14:- visionOS 27 beta specifically de-risks the nebula: **native Gaussian splat rendering** gives real volumetric depth instead of the flat-billboard workarounds you'd fight on 26.
16:- **Sharpest single risk: the hyperspace jump.** A forward star-rush is exactly the optic-flow pattern that triggers motion discomfort in a headset. It is buildable and can be made comfortable, but it needs deliberate mitigation (§7b) — it is the one feature most likely to make someone take the headset off.
23:- A single equirectangular sphere at "infinity" has **zero disparity and zero parallax** → the brain reads it as a painted dome. That is the "flat" failure you called out.
44:   │   │   │   │ L1 FAR BACKDROP      │    │    │   │  hi-res equirectangular Milky Way
54:| **L1 Far backdrop** | Fill the whole FOV so there are no gaps; base "stars everywhere" | none (it's the infinity plate) | Inward sphere, `UnlitMaterial`, hi-res equirectangular (§4) | Static or ultra-slow yaw |
55:| **L2 Nebula volume** | The signature look; the thing that must feel volumetric | Volumetric self-parallax + stereo | **Gaussian splats** (native RealityKit v27), fallback: additive soft particle clouds + a few billboard sheets | Color/emissive drift; very slow drift |
72:- **L1 backdrop:** equirectangular, target **8K–16K (8192×4096 → 16384×8192)**. 16K equirect is large in memory — mitigate with:
73:  - **Cubemap** (6 faces) instead of one equirect → better texel distribution, avoids pole pinch, easier to keep each face high-res.
112:- **Optional flavor:** slow pulsar blink, a distant "supernova" bloom (one-shot emissive flash + expanding shell), aurora-like curtains (v27 ships a Thórsmörk env with aurora — reference for look, not reusable asset).
138:- **`EnvironmentConfig`**: seed + nebula backend/palette + nebula density + star density/color mix + backdrop choice + phenomena rates + celestial body / asteroid-field selection (§7c–§7d). One struct fully describes a look.
147:Star Wars-style: stars stretch into radial streaks, rush past, then settle into the new environment. This is the **highest comfort-risk feature in the app** — spec it defensively.
153:4. **Arrival (0.4–0.8 s):** streaks decelerate and recontract into the **new** environment's star volume; new nebula/backdrop fade in.
171:- Sphere + high-res equirectangular planetary map (8K–16K). Single texture, no LOD tree — because the framing distance is fixed.
192:- NASA / JPL / USGS publish public-domain equirectangular maps for the Sun, every planet, and major moons (Earth Blue Marble and Black Marble, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn with rings, and more). Strong fit for a "Sol System" curated preset.
227:- **Physics:** asteroids inside an "active zone" near the vantage become dynamic rigid bodies (RealityKit `PhysicsBodyComponent` + `CollisionComponent`). Rocks on seeded crossing trajectories collide naturally; distant rocks stay on cheap kinematic drift and promote to full physics only when they enter the active zone.
287:| L1 equirectangular backdrop | ESO Milky Way panorama (S. Brunier), NASA/ESA star maps; or procedural (generate an 8K+ equirect PNG in Python) | **License + resolution must be verified before shipping — not yet checked.** Procedural avoids licensing entirely. |
291:| Planetary maps (§7c) | NASA/JPL/USGS public-domain equirectangular maps; or procedural | Per-asset license/attribution to confirm; NASA generally public domain |
299:- **Camera never moves.** Only distant objects move, slowly. Self-motion illusion in a headset causes discomfort fast.
312:- **Beta API churn:** signatures for the newest features (native Gaussian splats, projective lighting) can change between betas — expect rework.
314:- **Fallback path:** if a splat feature is unstable, L2 degrades gracefully to additive soft-particle clouds + billboard sheets on the same clock — the rest of the architecture is unaffected. Design L2 as a **swappable module** behind a protocol so the nebula backend can change without touching L1/L3/L4.
322:3. **Backdrop source:** real panorama (needs license check) vs. procedural generation?
341:| Project scaffold + Full Immersive Space + L1 backdrop | 0.5 day |

result.output:
# macOS
.DS_Store

# Xcode / SwiftPM
build/
DerivedData/
*.xcuserstate
xcuserdata/
*.xcodeproj/xcuserdata/
*.xcworkspace/xcuserdata/
.swiftpm/

# XcodeGen output — regenerate from project.yml, do not commit
Float.xcodeproj/

# Python bytecode from the agent-build-tools harness (regenerates per worktree)
__pycache__/
*.pyc

# Raw source imagery (huge equirect panoramas / nebula stills). Keep out of git;
# only the processed, compressed shipping assets under Resources/Textures/ are committed.
Float/Resources/Textures/_raw/
!Float/Resources/Textures/_raw/README.md

# NOTE: entire / .entire handling is account-specific (darthzen vs. other).
# Do NOT blanket-ignore .entire here — follow your entire workflow when you git-init locally.

# SwiftPM local package build artifacts
Packages/**/.build/

# Reality Composer Pro 3 standalone project (binary .tm_* format) — kept locally for the
# shelved stereo-360 material work; NOT part of the app build (see rcp3-beta-format-change memory).
/RealityKitContent.realitycomposerpro/

# Throwaway RCP USD exports
/sphere.usda/

# Root-level Apple Spatial source HEICs — the shipping copies live in Resources/Textures/Backdrop
/shutterstock_*_spatial.public.heic
=== _raw README ===
# _raw — source imagery (NOT committed)

Drop original downloads here: full-res equirectangular panoramas, nebula stills,
anything straight from NASA/ESO/ESA. This whole folder is **gitignored** so the
huge source files never bloat the entire-mirrored repo.

Workflow: download → drop here → we process/compress → the final shipping asset
lands in `../Backdrop/` or `../Nebula/` (ASTC/compressed, small) and *that* is
what gets committed.

Keep a note of each file's source URL + license so it can go in `../CREDITS.md`.
=== Backdrop README ===
# Backdrop — L1 far backdrop (§4)

Equirectangular panoramas for the inward `FarBackdrop` sphere (`DepthLayers.swift`).

**Format:**
- Equirectangular, **2:1 aspect** (e.g. 8192×4096 or 16384×8192).
- Ship as ASTC-compressed (§4). Source can be PNG/EXR/HEIC in `_raw/`; the
  committed asset here should be the compressed version.
- One seam runs down the back of the sphere — a proper 2:1 equirect hides it.

**License-clean sources:**
- NASA SVS "Deep Star Maps 2020" — public domain, purpose-built equirect starfield.
- ESO Milky Way panorama (Brunier / Guisard) — CC BY 4.0 (credit required).

Record source + license for every file in `../CREDITS.md`.

# Texture asset credits & licenses

Every bundled image asset is tracked here with its source, license, and the
attribution obligation it carries. **Public-domain** assets need no credit;
**CC BY 4.0** assets require a visible credit line in the shipping app.

---

## L1 backdrop (`Backdrop/`)

### Deep Star Maps 2020 — celestial, 8K equirectangular
- **File (raw):** `_raw/starmap_2020_8k.exr` — downloaded 2026-07-19
- **Dimensions / format:** 8192×4096, OpenEXR (HDR float, zip), 2:1 equirect
- **Source:** https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/vis/a000000/a004800/a004851/starmap_2020_8k.exr
- **Page:** https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/4851
- **Producer:** NASA/Goddard SFC Scientific Visualization Studio (released 2020-09-09)
- **Data:** 1.7B stars — Hipparcos-2, Tycho-2, Gaia DR2
- **License:** Public domain (NASA). No attribution required.
- **Suggested credit (courtesy):** "NASA/Goddard SVS. Gaia DR2: ESA/Gaia/DPAC."
- **Higher res available:** 16K / 32K / 64K EXR, and galactic-coordinate variants
  (`..._gal.exr`), same directory.
- **Pipeline:** EXR → tonemap/convert → ASTC; committed compressed asset lands in
  `Backdrop/`. (RealityKit can't load EXR directly.)

### Shutterstock royalty-free space skies — 8K equirectangular
Purchased royalty-free (Rick's Shutterstock license). Distinct backdrops the jump cycles
through with the NASA plate. Originals (12K–15K JPG) in `_raw/`; bundled as 8192×4096 HEIC.
- **License:** Shutterstock Standard royalty-free (per Rick's account). No public attribution
  required; usage per the Shutterstock license terms.
- `dual_nebula.heic` — shutterstock #2436644261 (red+blue dual nebula w/ bright star)
- `blue_filaments.heic` — shutterstock #2572761365 (cool blue filamentary field)
- `teal_orange.heic` — shutterstock #2626369537 (dense teal + orange nebula)
- `dark_dust.heic` — shutterstock #2651221149 (dark, sparse brown dust wisps)
- `pale_haze.heic` — shutterstock #2700631105 (soft pale-blue haze + star clusters)

---

## L2 nebula reference / sprite source (`Nebula/`)

> Note on use: sampling *colors* from these for the palette creates no derivative
> and carries no obligation. **Cutting them into sprite cards / textures that ship
> in the app IS a derivative work → the CC BY 4.0 credit line below must appear in
> the app.** Raw files not yet downloaded — URLs documented per Rick.

### Centaurus A (galaxy) — JWST / MIRI, mid-infrared
- **Flickr:** https://www.flickr.com/photos/nasawebbtelescope/55377597821/
- **Subject:** Centaurus A — dusty filaments, loops, warm-dust clouds (good palette
  + filament reference)
- **Credit:** NASA, ESA, CSA, STScI; Image Processing: Alyssa Pagan (STScI),
  Joseph DePasquale (STScI), Macarena Garcia Marin (ESA Office at STScI)
- **Posted:** 2026-07-06 (JWST 4th science anniversary)
- **License:** CC BY 4.0 (attribution required)
- **Original dimensions:** not stated on page — grab "Original" from Flickr sizes.

### Young Galaxy Cluster MACS J0553.4-3342 — JWST
- **Flickr:** https://www.flickr.com/photos/nasawebbtelescope/55380623885/
- **Subject:** merging galaxy cluster + gravitational-lensing arcs (mostly dark
  field w/ galaxies — weaker nebula reference than Centaurus A)
- **Credit:** ESA/Webb, NASA & CSA, S. Fujimoto
- **Posted:** 2026-07-07

## Deep-sky feature imagery — L3 far objects (`_raw/deepsky/`)

Full-resolution masters for the distant galaxies / nebulae that hang in the far field
(§7c hero objects, L3 billboards). All downloaded 2026-07-23. Rick supplied NASA SVS
pages as the starting point; **SVS re-serves downscaled derivatives** (mostly 3840×2160
video frames), so each master below was traced back to the originating ESA/Hubble or
STScI release, which is 2–9× larger. SVS page IDs are noted for cross-reference.

**License (all ESA/Hubble items):** CC BY 4.0 — attribution required, credit line
unaltered and kept adjacent to the image. NASA SVS / STScI items are public domain.
Per the ESA/Hubble FAQ, individuals named in credit lines do **not** need to be
contacted; that clearance is what separates these from the rejected Fujii image below.

| File | Object | Dimensions | Release |
|---|---|---|---|
| `carina_hh901_mystic_mountain_heic1007c.tif` | HH 901 pillar, Carina Nebula | 7281×7149 | heic1007c |
| `m16_pillars_visible_heic1501a.tif` | Pillars of Creation, M16 — visible | 6780×7071 | heic1501a |
| `m16_pillars_infrared_heic1501b.tif` | Pillars of Creation, M16 — infrared | 3249×3045 | heic1501b |
| `m8_lagoon_visible_8kx8k.png` | Lagoon Nebula, M8 — visible | 8000×8000 | STScI/SVS 30943 |
| `m8_lagoon_infrared_8kx8k.png` | Lagoon Nebula, M8 — infrared | 8000×8000 | STScI/SVS 30943 |
| `s106_snow_angel_heic1118a.tif` | Sharpless 2-106 "snow angel" | 4356×3202 | heic1118a |
| `m104_sombrero_opo0328a.tif` | Sombrero Galaxy, M104 | 11472×6429 | opo0328a |
| `m106_heic1302a_7910x6178.tif` | Messier 106 / NGC 4258 | 7910×6178 | heic1302a |
| `ngc4631_whale_potw1146a.tif` | Whale Galaxy, NGC 4631 (edge-on) | 8933×2434 | potw1146a |
| `m31_andromeda_phat_10k_heic1502a.tif` | Andromeda, M31 — PHAT mosaic | 10000×3197 | heic1502a |
| `milkyway_360_panorama_eso0932a.tif` | Milky Way 360° panorama | 6000×3000 | eso0932a |

### Credit lines (must ship verbatim if the asset ships)
- **HH 901 / Mystic Mountain** (heic1007c, SVS 30940) — https://esahubble.org/images/heic1007c/
  "NASA, ESA, M. Livio and the Hubble 20th Anniversary Team (STScI)"
- **M16 Pillars of Creation** (heic1501a/b, SVS 31007) — https://esahubble.org/images/heic1501a/
  "NASA, ESA and the Hubble Heritage Team (STScI/AURA)"
- **M8 Lagoon Nebula** (SVS 30943) — https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/30943/
  "NASA, ESA, and STScI" — public domain. Hubble's 28th-anniversary image.
  Note: these are the 8K×8K STScI source plates, *larger* than the ESA/Hubble
  release (heic1808a, 4782×6028), so SVS is the better source for this one.
- **Sharpless 2-106** (heic1118a, SVS 30682) — https://esahubble.org/images/heic1118a/
  "NASA & ESA"
- **M104 Sombrero** (opo0328a, SVS 30995) — https://esahubble.org/images/opo0328a/
  "NASA/ESA and The Hubble Heritage Team (STScI/AURA)"
- **M106** (heic1302a, SVS 31021) — https://esahubble.org/images/heic1302a/
  "NASA, ESA, the Hubble Heritage Team (STScI/AURA), and R. Gendler (for the Hubble
  Heritage Team); Acknowledgment: J. GaBany"
  ⚠️ Contains amateur data composited by Gendler/GaBany. It is an official ESA/Hubble
  release under CC BY 4.0 and the FAQ clears reuse, but the credit line is longer than
  usual and must not be trimmed.
- **NGC 4631 Whale** (potw1146a, SVS 31016) — https://esahubble.org/images/potw1146a/
  "NASA & ESA"
- **M31 Andromeda** (heic1502a, SVS 30990) — https://esahubble.org/images/heic1502a/
  "NASA, ESA, J. Dalcanton, B.F. Williams, L.C. Johnson (Univ. of Washington), the
  PHAT team, and R. Gendler"
  Note: this is the 10K publication TIFF. The true original is 69536×22230 (4.3 GB
  PSB) with a 40K TIFF (1.7 GB) intermediate — both available if 10K proves too soft.
  It covers ~⅓ of the disc, not the whole galaxy; for a full-disc M31 billboard the
  SVS 30990 wide view (NOAO ground-based optical, 3840×2160) is the fallback.
- **Milky Way 360° panorama** (eso0932a) — https://www.eso.org/public/images/eso0932a/
  "ESO/S. Brunier" — CC BY 4.0. GigaGalaxy Zoom project, 2009.

### ⚠️ eso0932a resolves the §287 / §11 open question — and it is too small
The spec (§287) lists the ESO Milky Way panorama as an L1 backdrop candidate with
"license + resolution must be verified before shipping". Now verified:
- **License: fine** — CC BY 4.0, credit "ESO/S. Brunier".
- **Resolution: fails the spec target.** The public original is **6000×3000**, well
  under §4's 8K–16K equirect requirement, and *below* the Deep Star Maps plate already
  bundled (8192×4096, with 16K/32K/64K available). The 800-megapixel version referenced
  in the press release is not published — ESO directs requests to Brunier privately.
- **Conclusion:** keep Deep Star Maps as the L1 plate. eso0932a is still useful as a
  *look* reference (it is real astrophotography of the band, in galactic coordinates,
  2:1 equirect and seam-clean) but should not be upscaled into a shipping backdrop.

### Survey note — the rest of the ESO 360° panorama archive is unusable here
https://www.eso.org/public/images/archive/category/360pano/ (459 images) was enumerated:
of 250 catalogued entries, 138 are true 2:1 equirectangular, and **all but eso0932a are
ground-level panoramas** — Paranal/La Silla/ALMA/Chajnantor sites, ESO HQ interiors, and
the ESO Supernova planetarium. Every one has a horizon and hardware in frame, which
breaks the "floating in deep space, no ground" premise. Several are enormous
(30000×15000) and tempting on paper; they are not candidates. Do not re-survey this
archive — eso0932a is the only deep-sky entry in it.

---

 M Float/Resources/Textures/CREDITS.md

content.text:
All 11 masters downloaded, dimension-verified, and visually confirmed to be the right objects. They're in `Float/Resources/Textures/_raw/deepsky/` (889 MB, gitignored per the `_raw/` rule).

## The main finding: SVS was serving you downscaled derivatives

Most of those SVS pages are *visualization* products — video frames from a Hubble "visible vs. infrared" morph reel, capped at 3840×2160. I traced each back to the originating ESA/Hubble or STScI release, which is 2–9× larger:

| Object | SVS max | What I got | Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pillars of Creation (31007) | 3840×2160 | **6780×7071** heic1501a | 5.8× |
| Sombrero M104 (30995) | 3840×2160 | **11472×6429** opo0328a | 8.9× |
| HH 901 / Mystic Mountain (30940) | 3840×2160 | **7281×7149** heic1007c | 6.3× |
| S106 Snow Angel (30682) | 1920×1080 | **4356×3202** heic1118a | 6.7× |
| Andromeda M31 (30990) | 3840×2160 | **10000×3197** heic1502a | 3.9× |
| Lagoon M8 (30943) | — | 8000×8000 (SVS *was* best here) | — |
| M106 (31021), Whale NGC 4631 (31016) | already full-res | same file, verified | — |

I also grabbed infrared companions for M16 and M8, since the visible/IR pair is what those SVS reels were built around and the IR versions read very differently as far-field objects.

The M106 `.tif` and the S106 PNG you'd already pulled to the repo root are folded in — the M106 one was already the full-res original, so I just filed it; the S106 was the 1920×1080 frame, replaced by the 4356×3202 master.

## Two things that change project decisions

**§287 / §11 open question is now answered — and the answer is no.** The ESO Milky Way panorama (eso0932a) is license-clean (CC BY 4.0, "ESO/S. Brunier") but the public original is only **6000×3000** — below §4's 8K–16K target and *smaller than the Deep Star Maps plate you already ship*. The 800-megapixel version from the press release was never published; ESO routes requests to Brunier privately. Keep Deep Star Maps as L1. I downloaded eso0932a anyway as a look reference — it's real astrophotography, galactic coordinates, seam-clean 2:1.

**The rest of the ESO 360pano archive is a dead end.** The listing is JS-rendered, so I parsed the embedded catalogue: 250 entries, 138 true 2:1 equirect — and every one except eso0932a is a ground panorama (Paranal, ALMA, La Silla, ESO HQ interiors, the Supernova planetarium). Horizon and hardware in every frame, which kills the no-ground premise. Some are 30000×15000 and look tempting in a size-sorted list; they aren't candidates. I wrote that into CREDITS.md so nobody re-surveys it.

## Practical note for L3 use

Sombrero, M106, Whale, S106 and the Pillars sit on black backgrounds — they key out cleanly for additive billboards. Carina/HH 901 and both Lagoon plates are edge-to-edge nebula with no black margin, so they'll need feathered masks or they'll show as visible rectangles.

CREDITS.md is updated with a new deep-sky section: per-file table, verbatim credit lines (I pulled the exact strings off each release page rather than trusting the SVS paraphrase — two of them differed), the CC BY 4.0 obligations, and the M106 caveat that its credit line includes composited amateur data from Gendler/GaBany and must not be trimmed.

That's the only tracked change; I haven't committed it.
I'm willing to loosen the on-ground gate if the focus really is on the sky and the stars. For someone who gets vertigo floating in space, it will probably be good to have some grounded options.

That's a real correction, and the reasoning is sound — a static horizon is one of the strongest vection suppressors there is. It gives the vestibular system a rest frame to anchor to, which is exactly what's missing when you're surrounded by drifting stars on all sides. Worth noting this doesn't actually conflict with §9; it reinforces what §9 is trying to protect.

Let me re-survey what I dismissed, and check a second source that may fit even better.

cd /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-rashford-Developer-Float/e2618adc-48c8-4631-880a-a25b0a36608e/scratchpad && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
h=open('eso_viewall.html').read()
i=h.find('var images = [')
blk=h[i:h.find('];',i)]
ents=re.findall(r"id: '([^']*)',\s*title: '(.*?)',\s*width: (\d+),\s*height: (\d+)", blk, re.S)
print("catalogued:", len(ents))
NIGHT=('milky way','night','airglow','star','sky','moon','laser','cosmic','aurora','dark','dusk','green light','arch')
BAD=('inside','interior','lab','office','hq','headquarters','room','hall','cafeteria','gym','garden','warehouse','workshop','reception','residencia','planetarium','supernova','auditorium','entrance','hangar','guesthouse','void','exhibition','rooftop','mirror','dome','council')
cands=[]
for id,t,w,hh in ents:
    tl=t.lower()
    if abs(int(w)/int(hh)-2.0)>0.02: continue
    if any(b in tl for b in BAD): continue
    if not any(n in tl for n in NIGHT): continue
    cands.append((int(w),int(hh),id,t))
cands.sort(key=lambda e:-e[0])
print("night-sky 2:1 candidates:", len(cands))
for w,hh,id,t in cands: print(f"{w:>6}x{hh:<6} {id:<50} {t}")
EOF

# Night Sky HDRIs on Poly Haven

Based on the webpage content provided, I cannot list specific night sky HDRIs with their names, maximum resolutions, or licensing details. The page shows "0 results" for the HDRIs: Night category.

The page displays the interface for browsing Poly Haven's HDRI collection but does not contain actual asset data, specifications, or information about whether these maps are full 360-degree equirectangular environments with ground elements included.

To find this information, you would need to:
- Visit the actual HDRIs collection page and filter by "Night"
- Check individual HDRI asset pages for resolution and licensing details
- Review Poly Haven's documentation at `docs.polyhaven.com` or consult their [API](/our-api) for technical specifications

input.command:
curl -s "https://api.polyhaven.com/assets?t=hdris&c=night" | python3 -c "
import sys,json
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
print('night HDRIs:',len(d))
rows=[]
for k,v in d.items():
    mx=v.get('max_resolution',[0,0])
    rows.append((mx[0], k, v.get('name'), ','.join(v.get('categories',[])), v.get('whitebalance')))
for w,k,n,c,_ in sorted(rows,key=lambda r:-r[0]):
    print(f'{w:>6}px  {k:<28} {n:<32} [{c}]')
" 2>&1 | head -40

input.command:
cd /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-rashford-Developer-Float/e2618adc-48c8-4631-880a-a25b0a36608e/scratchpad/cand && python3 - <<'EOF'
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw
import glob, os
Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS=None
def sheet(files, out, cols=2, cw=760):
    ch=cw//2
    rows=(len(files)+cols-1)//cols
    S=Image.new('RGB',(cols*cw, rows*(ch+20)),(12,12,14))
    d=ImageDraw.Draw(S)
    for i,f in enumerate(files):
        im=Image.open(f); im.thumbnail((cw,ch), Image.LANCZOS)
        x=(i%cols)*cw; y=(i//cols)*(ch+20)
        S.paste(im,(x,y+20))
        d.text((x+4,y+5), os.path.basename(f)[:78], fill=(200,210,230))
    S.save(out, quality=88)
    print(out, S.size)
sheet(sorted(glob.glob('eso__*'))[:8], 'sheet_eso_a.jpg')
sheet(sorted(glob.glob('eso__*'))[8:], 'sheet_eso_b.jpg')
sheet(sorted(glob.glob('ph__*')), 'sheet_ph.jpg')
EOF

        if max(row)<=6: black+=1
        else: break
    print(f"{os.path.basename(f):<52} {W/H:>7.3f} {100*black/512:>12.1f}% {'':>12}")

input.old_string:
### Survey note — the rest of the ESO 360° panorama archive is unusable here
https://www.eso.org/public/images/archive/category/360pano/ (459 images) was enumerated:
of 250 catalogued entries, 138 are true 2:1 equirectangular, and **all but eso0932a are
ground-level panoramas** — Paranal/La Silla/ALMA/Chajnantor sites, ESO HQ interiors, and
the ESO Supernova planetarium. Every one has a horizon and hardware in frame, which
breaks the "floating in deep space, no ground" premise. Several are enormous
(30000×15000) and tempting on paper; they are not candidates. Do not re-survey this
archive — eso0932a is the only deep-sky entry in it.

---
input.new_string:
### Survey note — the ESO 360° panorama archive
https://www.eso.org/public/images/archive/category/360pano/ (459 images) was enumerated
from the catalogue embedded in the `viewall` page: 250 entries recovered, 138 true 2:1
equirectangular. Exactly one (eso0932a) is deep-sky; every other is a ground-level
panorama — Paranal/La Silla/ALMA/Chajnantor sites, ESO HQ interiors, ESO Supernova.

⚠️ **This note originally concluded "all unusable — do not re-survey."** That was written
under the no-ground reading of the premise. Rick relaxed that gate on 2026-07-23 (a static
horizon is a vection rest frame and a vertigo mitigation, not a violation of §9), so the
night-sky subset is now live — see **Grounded night-sky backdrops** below. The interiors
and daytime shots remain out.

---

## Grounded night-sky backdrops (`_raw/grounded/`) — vertigo-mitigation option

For viewers who get vertigo in an all-sky float, a static horizon gives the vestibular
system a rest frame. These are full-sphere 2:1 equirects that drop straight onto the
existing `FarBackdrop` inward sphere with no code change.

### ⚠️ The trap: most ESO "-extended" panoramas are black-padded at the nadir
ESO pads partial-sphere captures out to a nominal 2:1 with **pure black fill**. The
catalogue reports 2.000 aspect for all of them, so this is invisible until you measure it.
Measured nadir black fraction across the night-sky candidates:

| Candidate | Nadir black | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| `150119-3p6-airglow-eq-extended` | 47.9% | ✗ reject |
| `150119cesta-airglow-eq-extended` | 46.5% | ✗ reject |
| `150123-24_atacama_fullfr_cc-eq-ext` | 44.7% | ✗ reject (also has an identifiable person) |
| `potw2514c` | 43.4% | ✗ reject |
| `uhd-9428-panorama-eq-extended` | 41.4% | ✗ reject |
| `150126vlt2-dal-jup-eq-extended` | 40.0% | ✗ reject |
| `nik9850p1-cc-extended` | 38.3% | ✗ reject |
| `potw1224a-ext` | 32.4% | ✗ reject |
| `2016_04_06_VISTA_night_Pano-VTversion_CC` | 29.3% | ✗ reject |
| `2016_04_09_ALMA_Central_Array_Reproj-CC` | 28.7% | ✗ reject |
| `2016-04-03-paranal-vlt-360fd-extended-cc` | 27.5% | ✗ reject |
| `brammer-nightmoon-eq-ext` | 8.6% | ✗ reject (twilight/moonlit, reads as daytime) |
| **`ESO_Paranal_360_Marcio_Cabral_Chile_09-CC`** | **0.0%** | ✓ **keep** |
| **`ESO_Paranal_360_Marcio_Cabral_Chile_10-CC`** | **0.0%** | ✓ **keep** |
| **`2019-06-30-FSLaSillaBTW-3p6-And-NTT-EQproj-CC`** | **0.0%** | ✓ **keep** |
| **`2019-07-02-ExTra-Airglow-360Pano-24mm-EQ-CC`** | **0.0%** | ✓ **keep** |

Rejected ones look down onto a black void with a hard circular edge — which destroys the
very rest frame the ground is there to provide. **Always measure the nadir before adopting
an ESO panorama.** Reproduce with the check in `scripts/` or: downsample to 64×512 luma,
count bottom rows where `max(row) <= 6`.

### Kept — ESO Atacama night panoramas (CC BY 4.0)
- `paranal_vlt_milkyway_cabral09_26k.jpg` — 26162×13081. Milky Way over the VLT platform.
  **Credit: "M. Cabral/ESO"** — https://www.eso.org/public/images/ESO_Paranal_360_Marcio_Cabral_Chile_09-CC/
- `paranal_vlt_milkyway_cabral10_26k.jpg` — 25786×12893. VLT with laser guide stars firing.
  **Credit: "M. Cabral/ESO"**
- `lasilla_milkyway_arc_2019_23k.jpg` — 23384×11692. Milky Way arc over La Silla.
  **Credit: "ESO/P. Horálek"** (verify on the release page before shipping)
- `lasilla_extra_airglow_2019_24k.jpg` — 23692×11846. ExTrA domes under airglow.
  **Credit: "ESO/P. Horálek"** (verify on the release page before shipping)

These are the `cdn.eso.org/images/large/<id>.jpg` files, which for this archive are served
at **full catalogue resolution** — not downscaled. The `original` TIFFs are the same pixels
at ~358 MB each; the JPEGs are the practical master.

⚠️ ESO CC BY 4.0 carries two live exceptions: the **ESO logo** is protected, and
**commercial use of images with identifiable people is prohibited**. All four kept images
were checked for people; `150123-24_atacama` was rejected partly on that basis. Telescope
domes carry ESO branding — fine as photographic content, but do not crop a logo out as a
standalone mark.

### Kept — Poly Haven night HDRIs (CC0, no attribution required)
True full-sphere, real HDR, no black padding, and **CC0** — the cleanest licensing in this
entire file. Downloaded at 8K to match §4's backdrop budget; 16K and 24K are available.
- `rogland_clear_night_8k.hdr` — 8192×4096 Radiance RGBE (24576×12288 max).
  Strong Milky Way arch over dark rocky desert, **no buildings or hardware**. Best fit if
  the goal is "grounded but not a specific real place."
  Author: Greg Zaal. https://polyhaven.com/a/rogland_clear_night
- `dikhololo_night_8k.hdr` — 8192×4096 Radiance RGBE (24576×12288 max).
  Milky Way over dark scrub with tree silhouettes; warmer, softer horizon glow.
  Author: Greg Zaal. https://polyhaven.com/a/dikhololo_night

Courtesy credit "HDRI from Poly Haven (CC0)" is appreciated but **not legally required**.

⚠️ Poly Haven's `night` tag is unreliable — over half the 59 tagged assets are moonlit or
twilight and read as blue daylight (`clarens_night_01`, `kloppenheim_02`,
`narrow_moonlit_road`, `qwantani_moonrise`). Preview before adopting; only the two above
survived a visual pass.

### Open design consequences of shipping a grounded mode
Not blockers, but a ground plane interacts with three existing systems:
1. **§7b hyperspace jump** — with a horizon present the jump must either keep the ground
   static (breaks the fiction) or move it (destroys the rest frame, and is *worse* for
   comfort than the current design). Grounded presets likely need the jump replaced with a
   cross-fade. This is the biggest one.
2. **§4 L2 nebula volume** — a surrounding splat/particle volume will clip through the
   horizon. Needs an above-horizon clamp in grounded presets.
3. **§7d drift + debris** — ambient objects and debris on paths below the horizon will read
   as passing "underground." Needs culling against the horizon plane.

---

content.text:
Six grounded candidates downloaded and verified, in `_raw/grounded/`. Two findings changed the shortlist substantially.

## Most ESO "extended" panoramas are black-padded — and the catalogue hides it

ESO pads partial-sphere captures out to a nominal 2:1 with pure black fill. Every one reports `aspect 2.000`, so it looks clean in metadata. I measured the actual nadir:

| | Nadir black |
|---|---|
| Airglow above the 3.6m, View above La Silla, Chilean sky | 45–48% |
| La Silla green light, Chajnantor, VLT night gems | 40–43% |
| VISTA at night, Night over Chajnantor, Milky Way glow at Paranal | 27–29% |
| **Cabral Paranal ×2, La Silla 2019 ×2** | **0%** |

Of 26 night candidates, **4 are true full-sphere**. The rest put a black void with a hard circular edge directly underfoot — which destroys the exact rest frame the ground is supposed to provide. The 30000×15000 VISTA shot is the most tempting on paper and is 29% black.

## Poly Haven is the better licensing story

CC0, no attribution at all, true HDR, up to 24576×12288. I pulled two at 8K:

- **`rogland_clear_night`** — strong Milky Way arch over dark rocky desert, *no buildings or hardware*. This is my pick if the goal is "grounded" without committing to a specific real place.
- **`dikhololo_night`** — warmer, tree silhouettes, softer horizon glow.

Caveat: Poly Haven's `night` tag is unreliable — over half of the 59 tagged assets are moonlit and read as blue daylight. Only these two survived a visual pass.

The ESO four are excellent astrophotography but they are unmistakably *Paranal* — VLT domes, roads, guardrails, laser guide stars. That's a real place under a real sky, which is a different experience from Float's premise.

## The fork worth deciding before I go further

There are three flavors of "grounded," and they're not the same product:

1. **Abstract platform in space** — keep every existing deep-space backdrop, add a static dark disc or subtle grid below the viewer. Preserves the floating-in-space premise, gives the vestibular rest frame, works with *all* current backdrops, needs no new assets or licensing, and is one entity on `UniverseRoot`. **This is what I'd recommend trying first** — it's the cheapest test of whether a rest frame actually fixes the vertigo, and if it does, the terrestrial panoramas become optional flavor rather than a dependency.
2. **Natural night landscape** — `rogland_clear_night`. Grounded, CC0, no hardware.
3. **Real observatory** — the ESO Paranal/La Silla set. Most spectacular sky, most specific place.

Three things a horizon touches, none blocking but all real: the **§7b jump** is the significant one — with a ground present it either stays static (breaks the fiction) or moves (destroys the rest frame, worse for comfort than today), so grounded presets probably want a cross-fade instead of a star-rush. The **L2 nebula** will clip through the horizon and needs an above-horizon clamp, and **§7d drift/debris** below the horizon will read as passing underground.

CREDITS.md has all of it — the nadir measurements with the reproduction method, verified credit lines (ESO writes these photographer-first, "M. Cabral/ESO", not the other way round), the CC BY 4.0 exceptions for logos and identifiable people, and the design consequences above. I also went back and amended my earlier "do not re-survey this archive" note, which was written under the old gate and would have been misleading.

Only CREDITS.md is tracked; nothing committed.

Let's pull all these and add them. I also want to update the selector interface to have a way to select the view. As the library grows, I want to be able to quickly and easily get to a specific scene I want to test.
Let me look at how backdrops and the control panel work today before changing anything.

49	    enum Kind: String, Codable { case proceduralPlanet, namedBody, star }
50	    var kind: Kind = .proceduralPlanet
51	    var named: String? = nil                 // e.g. "Saturn" for the Sol System preset
52	    var hasRings: Bool = false
53	    var ringsResolveClose: Bool = true       // §7c two-tier rings
54	    var atmosphereTint: SIMD3<Float> = [0.4, 0.6, 1.0]
55	    var distance: Float = 90                 // §3 hero distance ~40–150 m
56	}
57	
58	/// §7d asteroid field.
59	struct AsteroidFieldConfig: Codable, Equatable {
60	    var count: Int = 300
61	    var safetyRadius: Float = 2.5            // §7d/§9 exclusion bubble — never violated
62	    var enableImpacts: Bool = true          // §7d emergent layer (free-running)
63	    var tameRocksForFlicking: Int = 3       // §7f reachable rocks (open decision §11 #14)
64	}

64	    /// flash so the change is masked. A stepping stone to the full streak sequence.
65	    func newEnvironment() {
66	        // "New Environment" acts on whatever you're looking at: cycle the imported skies, or
67	        // jump the generated universe.
68	        if environmentSource == .importedSpatial { advanceImportedImage(); return }
69	        guard let whiteout, sceneContainer != nil else { return }
70	        // Don't retrigger while a flash is already running.
71	        if whiteout.components[WhiteoutComponent.self]?.active == true { return }
72	        var c = whiteout.components[WhiteoutComponent.self] ?? WhiteoutComponent()
73	        c.active = true; c.elapsed = 0
74	        whiteout.components.set(c)
75	
76	        // Swap at peak white (single-await Task — the reliable pattern). ~fadeIn + a bit.
77	        Task { @MainActor in
78	            try? await Task.sleep(for: .seconds(0.30))
79	            applyNewEnvironment()
80	        }
81	    }
82	
83	    /// LIGHT in-place swap: full-scene rebuilds (~6k star entities) crashed the device render
84	    /// server, so keep the star field and only re-orient the backdrop + rebuild the
85	    /// (few-hundred-entity) nebula. Deterministic seed chain reproduces (§7e).
86	    private func applyNewEnvironment() {
87	        guard let container = sceneContainer,
88	              let root = container.children.first(where: { $0.name == "UniverseRoot" }) else { return }
89	        let nextSeed = currentConfig.seed &* 2862933555777941757 &+ 3037000493
90	        var newConfig = EnvironmentConfig.random(seed: nextSeed)
91	
92	        // Cycle backdrops through a shuffle-bag so New Environment visits EVERY panorama
93	        // before any repeats — independent random draws (even unbiased) clustered on a few
94	        // skies. Overrides the seed-derived backdrop; density is re-derived for the pick so
95	        // the §7a busyness suppression still applies.
96	        var pick = SeededRandom(seed: nextSeed ^ 0x424B_4452_5348_4646)   // "BKDRSHFF"
97	        newConfig.backdrop = nextBackdrop(rng: &pick)
98	        newConfig.nebulaDensity = pick.unit() * FarBackdrop.nebulaScale(forBackdrop: newConfig.backdrop)
99	
100	        currentConfig = newConfig
101	        let gen = EnvironmentGenerator(config: newConfig)
102	
103	        // Backdrop: reskin in place — new panorama (config.backdrop) + orientation + tint.
104	        // Loads ONE 8K texture (light — not a full-scene rebuild), hidden by the whiteout.
105	        if let backdrop = root.findEntity(named: "L1_Backdrop") as? ModelEntity {
106	            FarBackdrop.reskin(backdrop, gen: gen)
107	        }
108	
109	        // Nebula: rebuild just this layer (keep the heavy star field untouched).
110	        root.children.filter { $0.name.hasPrefix("L2_Nebula") }.forEach { $0.removeFromParent() }
111	        root.addChild(NebulaVolume.make(gen: gen))
112	    }
113	
114	    /// Shuffle-bag over the backdrops: hand out each panorama once (in a seeded random
115	    /// order) before refilling, so New Environment cycles through ALL of them. Refill avoids
116	    /// opening on the sky we just showed, so there are no back-to-back repeats across bags.
117	    private var backdropBag: [Int] = []
118	    private func nextBackdrop(rng: inout SeededRandom) -> Int {
119	        let count = FarBackdrop.textureNames.count
120	        if backdropBag.isEmpty {
121	            var bag = Array(0..<count)
122	            for i in stride(from: count - 1, through: 1, by: -1) {      // Fisher–Yates
123	                let j = Int(rng.next() % UInt64(i + 1))
124	                bag.swapAt(i, j)
125	            }
126	            // removeLast() serves the pick, so keep bag.last (the next pick) off the current sky.
127	            if count > 1 && bag.last == currentConfig.backdrop { bag.swapAt(0, count - 1) }
128	            backdropBag = bag
129	        }
130	        return backdropBag.removeLast()
131	    }
132	}
133	
134	/// Minimal launcher; the real experience is the immersive space.
135	struct LauncherView: View {

137	    @Environment(\.openImmersiveSpace) private var openImmersiveSpace
138	    @Environment(\.openWindow) private var openWindow
139

143	            Text("Step into deep space.").foregroundStyle(.secondary)
144	            Button("Enter") {

148	                    model.immersion = .open

156	                .buttonStyle(.bordered)

158	
159	            // A/B toggle: imported Apple Spatial image  ⇄  generated scene.
160	            Button(model.environmentSource == .generated ? "Show Imported Image" : "Show Generated Scene") {
161	                model.toggleEnvironmentSource()

6	// L1 — §4. Inward sphere, unlit hi-res equirectangular. Effectively at infinity.
7	enum FarBackdrop {
8	    // Base equirect panoramas in the bundle — NASA SVS Deep Star Maps (public domain) plus
9	    // Shutterstock RF space skies (see CREDITS.md). config.backdrop picks the base; per-seed
10	    // rotation + a subtle color wash vary it further so jumps rarely repeat the same sky (§7a).
11	    static let textureNames = [
12	        "deep_star_map_8k", "dual_nebula", "blue_filaments", "teal_orange", "dark_dust", "pale_haze"
13	    ]
14	
15	    // §7a — how much real nebulosity/structure each panorama already carries (0 = near-black
16	    // star field, 1 = nebula-filled sky). MEASURED from the panoramas (blurred-luminance mean
17	    // + large-scale variation + non-black coverage), not eyeballed. Index-aligned with
18	    // textureNames. Drives generated-nebula density down (below) so our procedural gas doesn't
19	    // pile onto — and thereby give away — an already-busy real sky.
20	    static let busyness: [Float] = [0.00, 0.80, 0.89, 0.65, 0.28, 0.93]
21	
22	    // Multiplier applied to a config's nebula density for a given backdrop: a quiet sky keeps
23	    // the full range, a busy sky gets a whisper. The freed-up depth cue is the (unchanged,
24	    // dense) L3 star field showing through more clearly — see AppModel.applyNewEnvironment.
25	    static func nebulaScale(forBackdrop index: Int) -> Float {
26	        let n = textureNames.count
27	        let b = busyness[((index % n) + n) % n]
28	        return 1.0 - 0.85 * b            // busy=0 → 1.0, busy=0.93 → ~0.21
29	    }

31	    @MainActor
32	    static func make(gen: EnvironmentGenerator) -> Entity {
33	        let mesh = MeshResource.generateSphere(radius: 1000)
34	        var material = UnlitMaterial()
35	        material.color = .init(tint: .init(white: 0.01, alpha: 1.0), texture: nil)  // near-black void until the texture loads
36	        let e = ModelEntity(mesh: mesh, materials: [material])
37	        e.name = "L1_Backdrop"
38	        e.scale.z = -1  // flip winding so the inside surface renders
39	        reskin(e, gen: gen)
40	        return e
41	    }
42	
43	    // Pick the panorama, orientation, and tint for this environment and apply them to an
44	    // existing backdrop sphere — used both at build time and on a jump (§7b). Loads on
45	    // demand (no cache): replacing the material drops the old texture, so only ~1 8K
46	    // panorama is resident at a time (6 cached would blow the GPU budget).
47	    @MainActor
48	    static func reskin(_ e: ModelEntity, gen: EnvironmentGenerator) {
49	        var rng = gen.backdropStream()
50	        let idx = ((gen.config.backdrop % textureNames.count) + textureNames.count) % textureNames.count
51	        let name = textureNames[idx]

58	            sqrt(u1) * sin(2 * pi * u3),     sqrt(u1) * cos(2 * pi * u3)))
59	
60	        // Subtle per-seed color wash (mostly white, slight cast).
61	        let tint = UIColor(red: CGFloat(0.82 + 0.18 * rng.unit()),
62	                           green: CGFloat(0.82 + 0.18 * rng.unit()),
63	                           blue: CGFloat(0.82 + 0.18 * rng.unit()), alpha: 1)
64

67	                print("[Float] L1 backdrop texture '\(name)' not found")
68	                return
69	            }
70	            var lit = UnlitMaterial()

79	struct WhiteoutComponent: Component {
80	    var active = false
81	    var elapsed: TimeInterval = 0
82	    var fadeIn: TimeInterval = 0.25
83	    var hold: TimeInterval = 0.12
84	    var fadeOut: TimeInterval = 0.45
85	    var total: TimeInterval { fadeIn + hold + fadeOut }
86	}

89	    static let query = EntityQuery(where: .has(WhiteoutComponent.self))
90	    init(scene: Scene) {}
91	    func update(context: SceneUpdateContext) {
92	        MainActor.assumeIsolated {
93	            for e in context.entities(matching: Self.query, updatingSystemWhen: .rendering) {
94	                guard var c = e.components[WhiteoutComponent.self], c.active,
95	                      let me = e as? ModelEntity else { continue }
96	                c.elapsed += context.deltaTime
97	                var a: Float
98	                if c.elapsed < c.fadeIn {
99	                    a = Float(c.elapsed / c.fadeIn)                                   // fade to white
100	                } else if c.elapsed < c.fadeIn + c.hold {
101	                    a = 1                                                            // peak (swap happens here)
102	                } else if c.elapsed < c.total {
103	                    a = Float(1 - (c.elapsed - c.fadeIn - c.hold) / c.fadeOut)         // reveal new env
104	                } else {
105	                    a = 0; c.active = false
106	                }
107	                a = max(0, min(1, a))
108	                var mat = UnlitMaterial()
109	                mat.color = .init(tint: UIColor(white: 1, alpha: CGFloat(a)), texture: nil)
110	                mat.blending = .transparent(opacity: 1.0)

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Backdrop asset tooling for Float's L1 far backdrop (§4).

Two jobs, both of which were previously done by hand and do not scale as the
panorama library grows:

  ingest   raw panorama (JPEG / Radiance .hdr / PNG / TIFF) -> 8192x4096 HEIC
           in Resources/Textures/Backdrop/, with an explicit tonemap for HDR
           sources so night skies stay night (sips' default curve lifts the
           ground until it reads as dusk).

  measure  report the numbers FarBackdrop needs per panorama:
             busyness  0 = near-black star field, 1 = nebula-filled sky.
                       Blurred-luminance mean + large-scale variation +
                       non-black coverage, matching the scale the original six
                       were tuned on (see CREDITS.md / DepthLayers.swift).
             nadir     fraction of the bottom of the frame that is pure black.
                       ESO pads partial-sphere panoramas out to a nominal 2:1
                       with black fill; anything above a few percent looks down
                       into a void with a hard circular edge. Reject those.
             horizon   fraction of frame height below the brightness step, i.e.
                       how much of the sphere is ground. 0 => no horizon =>
                       a space sky, not a grounded one.

Usage:
    python3 scripts/backdrop_tool.py measure Float/Resources/Textures/Backdrop/*.heic
    python3 scripts/backdrop_tool.py ingest  --exposure -1.5 raw.hdr out_name
"""

import argparse
import os
import subprocess
import sys
import tempfile

import numpy as np
from PIL import Image

Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS = None

TARGET_W, TARGET_H = 8192, 4096
BACKDROP_DIR = os.path.join(
    os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))),
    "Float", "Resources", "Textures", "Backdrop",
)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------- loading

def _load_radiance(path):
    """Read a Radiance RGBE .hdr into a float32 linear RGB array."""
    import imageio.v3 as iio
    return np.asarray(iio.imread(path), dtype=np.float32)


def load_linear(path):
    """Load any supported source as float32 linear RGB in roughly [0, inf)."""
    if path.lower().endswith((".hdr", ".exr")):
        return _load_radiance(path)
    im = Image.open(path).convert("RGB")
    srgb = np.asarray(im, dtype=np.float32) / 255.0
    # sRGB -> linear so the tonemap below operates in the same space for all inputs.
    return np.where(srgb <= 0.04045, srgb / 12.92, ((srgb + 0.055) / 1.055) ** 2.4)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------- tonemap

def tonemap(lin, exposure=0.0, gamma=1.0, black=0.0):
    """Exposure (stops) -> optional black lift/crush -> Reinhard -> sRGB encode.

    Reinhard rather than a hard clip: night panoramas carry a very bright Milky
    Way core and moon against a near-black ground, and clipping blows the core
    into a flat white blob that reads as a light leak on the inside of a sphere.
    """
    x = lin * (2.0 ** exposure)
    if black:
        x = np.clip(x - black, 0.0, None)
    x = x / (1.0 + x)                                  # Reinhard
    if gamma != 1.0:
        x = np.clip(x, 0.0, 1.0) ** gamma
    x = np.clip(x, 0.0, 1.0)
    srgb = np.where(x <= 0.0031308, x * 12.92, 1.055 * (x ** (1 / 2.4)) - 0.055)
    return (np.clip(srgb, 0, 1) * 255.0 + 0.5).astype(np.uint8)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------- measuring

def _luma(rgb_u8):
    a = rgb_u8.astype(np.float32) / 255.0
    return 0.2126 * a[..., 0] + 0.7152 * a[..., 1] + 0.0722 * a[..., 2]


def measure(path):
    """Return (busyness, nadir_black_fraction, ground_fraction) for a panorama."""
    im = Image.open(path).convert("RGB")
    W, H = im.size

    # --- nadir black fill: full-width rows at the very bottom that are pure black.
    tall = np.asarray(im.resize((64, 512), Image.BOX), dtype=np.uint8)
    black_rows = 0
    for y in range(511, -1, -1):
        if tall[y].max() <= 6:
            black_rows += 1
        else:
            break
    nadir = black_rows / 512.0

    # --- busyness: work at a small size so this measures large-scale structure
    # (nebulosity, the Milky Way band) and not per-star noise.
    small = np.asarray(im.resize((256, 128), Image.LANCZOS), dtype=np.uint8)
    L = _luma(small)
    mean = float(L.mean())
    variation = float(L.std())
    coverage = float((L > 0.06).mean())          # fraction that isn't void
    busyness = float(np.clip(1.9 * mean + 1.1 * variation + 0.45 * coverage, 0, 1))

    # --- ground fraction: scan up from the bottom for the horizon, i.e. the row
    # where the row-mean brightness steps up into sky. Ground in these panoramas
    # is consistently darker and much flatter than the sky above it.
    rows = L.mean(axis=1)
    valid = rows[: int(len(rows) * (1 - nadir))] if nadir < 0.95 else rows
    ground = 0.0
    if len(valid) > 8:
        bottom_half = valid[len(valid) // 2:]
        if bottom_half.size:
            step = np.diff(bottom_half)
            k = int(np.argmax(step)) if step.size else 0
            # Only call it a horizon if the step is a real discontinuity.
            if step.size and step[k] > 0.5 * float(bottom_half.std() + 1e-6) and step[k] > 0.004:
                ground = (len(valid) - (len(valid) // 2 + k)) / float(len(rows))
    return busyness, nadir, ground


# ---------------------------------------------------------------- commands

def cmd_measure(args):
    print(f"{'file':<34}{'busyness':>10}{'nadir':>9}{'ground':>9}   note")
    for p in args.paths:
        try:
            b, n, g = measure(p)
        except Exception as e:                                    # noqa: BLE001
            print(f"{os.path.basename(p):<34}  !! {e}")
            continue
        note = []
        if n > 0.03:
            note.append(f"REJECT black nadir {n:.0%}")
        if g > 0.05:
            note.append(f"grounded (~{g:.0%} ground)")
        print(f"{os.path.basename(p):<34}{b:>10.2f}{n:>9.1%}{g:>9.1%}   {', '.join(note)}")


def cmd_ingest(args):
    lin = load_linear(args.src)
    rgb = tonemap(lin, exposure=args.exposure, gamma=args.gamma, black=args.black)
    im = Image.fromarray(rgb, "RGB")
    if im.size != (TARGET_W, TARGET_H):
        im = im.resize((TARGET_W, TARGET_H), Image.LANCZOS)

    os.makedirs(BACKDROP_DIR, exist_ok=True)
    out = os.path.join(BACKDROP_DIR, args.name + ".heic")
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".png", delete=False) as tf:
        tmp = tf.name
    try:
        im.save(tmp)
        subprocess.run(
            ["sips", "-s", "format", "heic", "-s", "formatOptions", str(args.quality),
             tmp, "--out", out],
            check=True, capture_output=True,
        )
    finally:
        os.unlink(tmp)

    b, n, g = measure(out)
    size_mb = os.path.getsize(out) / 1e6
    print(f"{args.name}.heic  {TARGET_W}x{TARGET_H}  {size_mb:.1f} MB   "
          f"busyness={b:.2f} nadir={n:.1%} ground={g:.1%}")
    return 0


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__,
                                 formatter_class=argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter)
    sub = ap.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)

    m = sub.add_parser("measure", help="report busyness / nadir / ground for panoramas")
    m.add_argument("paths", nargs="+")
    m.set_defaults(func=cmd_measure)

    i = sub.add_parser("ingest", help="convert a raw panorama into a bundled 8K HEIC")
    i.add_argument("src")
    i.add_argument("name", help="bundle name, no extension (must match FarBackdrop)")
    i.add_argument("--exposure", type=float, default=0.0, help="stops, HDR sources")
    i.add_argument("--gamma", type=float, default=1.0, help=">1 darkens midtones")
    i.add_argument("--black", type=float, default=0.0, help="linear black-point subtract")
    i.add_argument("--quality", type=int, default=80)
    i.set_defaults(func=cmd_ingest)

    args = ap.parse_args()
    sys.exit(args.func(args) or 0)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import numpy as np, sys
sys.path.insert(0,'scripts')
from backdrop_tool import open_rgb, _luma
from PIL import Image
names=["deep_star_map_8k","dual_nebula","blue_filaments","teal_orange","dark_dust","pale_haze"]
tuned=np.array([0.00,0.80,0.89,0.65,0.28,0.93])
rows=[]
for n in names:
    im=open_rgb(f"Float/Resources/Textures/Backdrop/{n}.heic")
    L=_luma(np.asarray(im.resize((256,128), Image.LANCZOS),dtype=np.uint8))
    rows.append([L.mean(), L.std(), (L>0.06).mean()])
    print(f"{n:<20} mean={L.mean():.4f} std={L.std():.4f} cov={(L>0.06).mean():.4f}")
A=np.array(rows); X=np.hstack([A, np.ones((len(A),1))])
coef,*_=np.linalg.lstsq(X,tuned,rcond=None)
pred=np.clip(X@coef,0,1)
print("\ncoef (mean, std, coverage, bias):", np.round(coef,4))
print("pred :", np.round(pred,3))
print("tuned:", tuned)
print("max abs err:", round(float(np.abs(pred-tuned).max()),4))
EOF

content.text: imageio needs an OpenCV backend for Radiance. I'll parse RGBE directly rather than add a dependency.

input.new_string:
def _load_radiance(path):
    """Read a Radiance RGBE .hdr into a float32 linear RGB array.

    Parsed here rather than via imageio, whose .hdr support needs an OpenCV
    backend we would otherwise not depend on. Handles the new-style
    per-component RLE scanlines that every modern writer emits, and falls back
    to flat RGBE for old-style files.
    """
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        data = f.read()

    # Header: text lines terminated by a blank line, then the resolution line.
    pos = 0
    while True:
        eol = data.index(b"\n", pos)
        line = data[pos:eol]
        pos = eol + 1
        if line.strip() == b"":
            break
    eol = data.index(b"\n", pos)
    res = data[pos:eol].split()
    pos = eol + 1
    if len(res) != 4 or res[0] != b"-Y" or res[2] != b"+X":
        raise ValueError(f"unsupported Radiance resolution line: {res!r}")
    H, W = int(res[1]), int(res[3])

    buf = np.frombuffer(data, dtype=np.uint8, offset=pos)
    rgbe = np.empty((H, W, 4), dtype=np.uint8)
    p = 0
    for y in range(H):
        if (W >= 8 and W < 32768 and buf[p] == 2 and buf[p + 1] == 2
                and (int(buf[p + 2]) << 8 | int(buf[p + 3])) == W):
            p += 4                                        # new-style RLE
            for c in range(4):
                x = 0
                while x < W:
                    n = int(buf[p]); p += 1
                    if n > 128:                            # run
                        rgbe[y, x:x + n - 128, c] = buf[p]; p += 1
                        x += n - 128
                    else:                                  # literal
                        rgbe[y, x:x + n, c] = buf[p:p + n]; p += n
                        x += n
        else:                                              # old-style flat
            rgbe[y] = buf[p:p + W * 4].reshape(W, 4)
            p += W * 4

    e = rgbe[..., 3].astype(np.int32)
    scale = np.where(e == 0, 0.0, np.exp2(e - 136)).astype(np.float32)
    return rgbe[..., :3].astype(np.float32) * scale[..., None]

20	}

63	    /// Jump to a fresh environment (§7b): flash the whiteout, and swap under the peak of the
64	    /// flash so the change is masked. A stepping stone to the full streak sequence.
65	    func newEnvironment() {
66	        // "New Environment" acts on whatever you're looking at: cycle the imported skies, or
67	        // jump the generated universe.
68	        if environmentSource == .importedSpatial { advanceImportedImage(); return }
69	        guard let whiteout, sceneContainer != nil else { return }
70	        // Don't retrigger while a flash is already running.
71	        if whiteout.components[WhiteoutComponent.self]?.active == true { return }
72	        var c = whiteout.components[WhiteoutComponent.self] ?? WhiteoutComponent()
73	        c.active = true; c.elapsed = 0
74	        whiteout.components.set(c)
75	
76	        // Swap at peak white (single-await Task — the reliable pattern). ~fadeIn + a bit.
77	        Task { @MainActor in
78	            try? await Task.sleep(for: .seconds(0.30))
79	            applyNewEnvironment()
80	        }
81	    }
82	
83	    /// LIGHT in-place swap: full-scene rebuilds (~6k star entities) crashed the device render
84	    /// server, so keep the star field and only re-orient the backdrop + rebuild the
85	    /// (few-hundred-entity) nebula. Deterministic seed chain reproduces (§7e).
86	    private func applyNewEnvironment() {
87	        guard let container = sceneContainer,
88	              let root = container.children.first(where: { $0.name == "UniverseRoot" }) else { return }
89	        let nextSeed = currentConfig.seed &* 2862933555777941757 &+ 3037000493
90	        var newConfig = EnvironmentConfig.random(seed: nextSeed)
91	
92	        // Cycle backdrops through a shuffle-bag so New Environment visits EVERY panorama
93	        // before any repeats — independent random draws (even unbiased) clustered on a few
94	        // skies. Overrides the seed-derived backdrop; density is re-derived for the pick so
95	        // the §7a busyness suppression still applies.
96	        var pick = SeededRandom(seed: nextSeed ^ 0x424B_4452_5348_4646)   // "BKDRSHFF"
97	        newConfig.backdrop = nextBackdrop(rng: &pick)
98	        newConfig.nebulaDensity = pick.unit() * FarBackdrop.nebulaScale(forBackdrop: newConfig.backdrop)
99	
100	        currentConfig = newConfig
101	        let gen = EnvironmentGenerator(config: newConfig)
102	
103	        // Backdrop: reskin in place — new panorama (config.backdrop) + orientation + tint.
104	        // Loads ONE 8K texture (light — not a full-scene rebuild), hidden by the whiteout.
105	        if let backdrop = root.findEntity(named: "L1_Backdrop") as? ModelEntity {
106	            FarBackdrop.reskin(backdrop, gen: gen)
107	        }
108	
109	        // Nebula: rebuild just this layer (keep the heavy star field untouched).
110	        root.children.filter { $0.name.hasPrefix("L2_Nebula") }.forEach { $0.removeFromParent() }
111	        root.addChild(NebulaVolume.make(gen: gen))
112	    }

content.text: One honest caveat to record — the `ground` heuristic misses `lasilla_airglow`, which is grounded (I confirmed visually; 0% nadir, real rocky foreground). Its horizon step is gradual, so the detector can't see it.

input.old_string:
horizon   fraction of frame height below the brightness step, i.e.
                       how much of the sphere is ground. 0 => no horizon =>
                       a space sky, not a grounded one.
input.new_string:
ground    fraction of frame height below the brightness step, i.e.
                       how much of the sphere is ground.

                       ADVISORY ONLY — it looks for a brightness discontinuity,
                       so it misses panoramas whose horizon fades gradually
                       (ground_lasilla_airglow is grounded but reports 0.0%).
                       `nadir` is the reliable automated check; the `grounded`
                       flag in FarBackdrop.catalog is set by eye, not from this.

input.new_string:
### Shipped assets (bundled 2026-07-23)
All six are ingested to **8192×4096 HEIC** in `Backdrop/` via
`scripts/backdrop_tool.py ingest`, which is reproducible — rerun the exact command to
regenerate. `busyness` below is the measured value carried in `FarBackdrop.catalog`.

| Bundled asset | Source | Ingest command | busyness |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ground_rogland_night.heic` | `rogland_clear_night_8k.hdr` | `ingest --exposure -3.0` | 0.55 |
| `ground_dikhololo_night.heic` | `dikhololo_night_8k.hdr` | `ingest --exposure -1.0` | 0.63 |
| `ground_paranal_vlt.heic` | `paranal_vlt_milkyway_cabral09_26k.jpg` | `ingest` | 0.84 |
| `ground_paranal_lasers.heic` | `paranal_vlt_milkyway_cabral10_26k.jpg` | `ingest` | 0.65 |
| `ground_lasilla_arc.heic` | `lasilla_milkyway_arc_2019_23k.jpg` | `ingest` | 0.89 |
| `ground_lasilla_airglow.heic` | `lasilla_extra_airglow_2019_24k.jpg` | `ingest` | 1.00 |

The two HDRIs need **negative** exposure: they are real HDR, and an untonemapped
(or sips-default) conversion lifts the ground until the scene reads as dusk. Exposure
was picked so the ground stays *readable* rather than crushed to silhouette — a black
ground gives a horizon line but no texture, which weakens the rest frame that is the
whole reason these are here. `dikhololo` was captured darker and needed +2 stops
relative to `rogland`.

content.text:
Done — six grounded scenes bundled, selector shipped, **BUILD SUCCEEDED** against the visionOS 27 SDK. Worth flagging: `CLAUDE.md` says the scaffold "has not been compiled." It compiles now, with one pre-existing deprecation warning (`DepthLayers.swift:605`, `TextureResource.generate(from:)`).

## Assets

All six ingested to 8192×4096 HEIC (2.6–6.9 MB each), verified present in the built `.app`, with `_raw/` correctly excluded from the bundle.

The two Poly Haven HDRIs needed **negative** exposure — they're real HDR, and both `sips`' default curve and a neutral tonemap lift the ground until the scene reads as dusk rather than night. I tuned exposure so the ground stays *readable* instead of crushing to silhouette: a black ground gives you a horizon line but no texture, which weakens the very rest frame these exist to provide. `dikhololo` came out too dark at the same setting and needed +2 stops.

`scripts/backdrop_tool.py` does ingest and measurement, so this is reproducible rather than a one-off. Its busyness coefficients are a least-squares fit against the six values you'd already hand-tuned in `DepthLayers` — max residual 0.047, so new panoramas land on the same scale as the originals. My first hand-picked formula read dark skies 0.2 high; the fit shows the coverage term was the culprit and belongs at ~zero weight. It parses Radiance RGBE directly rather than adding an OpenCV dependency for imageio.

## Two things I changed beyond the ask, both load-bearing

**Grounded skies get yaw-only rotation.** `reskin` applied a uniform-random quaternion to every backdrop. On a grounded panorama that tilts or inverts the horizon — and a *tilted* horizon is worse than none at all, because it confidently asserts an "up" that contradicts the inner ear. That's precisely the failure these are meant to prevent, so grounded skies now vary compass heading only.

**Grounded skies are excluded from random jumps.** The L2 nebula is a full surround and would hang under the ground, so `nebulaScale` returns 0 for grounded (with a TODO to clamp the volume to the upper hemisphere and restore a whisper). And a random jump *out of* a grounded sky drops the rest frame someone deliberately chose, while a jump *into* one drops a desert under a deep-space float — wrong in both directions. They're reachable only by explicit selection.

## Selector

`FarBackdrop`'s index-aligned `textureNames` + `busyness` arrays are gone — that's exactly the pattern that breaks as a library grows, since the two had to be edited in lockstep and would silently mis-scale the nebula if they drifted. One `catalog` of `Panorama` rows now carries name, title, busyness, and grounded.

`SceneSelectorView` is its own window (`"scenes"`, same pattern as the reader) so you can open it from inside the immersive space and leave it floating. Sections for Deep Space / Grounded / Imported, searchable, checkmark on the current scene, and each row surfaces its nebula-suppression state so "why is there no nebula here" has an on-screen answer. Selection reuses the §7b whiteout mask, so jumping to a scene is no less comfortable than jumping randomly — I factored that flash logic into one `maskedSwap` since two paths now need it.

## One caveat

The `ground` metric in the tool is advisory. It looks for a brightness discontinuity and misses `ground_lasilla_airglow`, whose horizon fades gradually — it reports 0% ground but is genuinely grounded (0% nadir, real rocky foreground, confirmed visually). `nadir` is the reliable automated check; the `grounded` flag is set by eye. That's documented in the script rather than left as a trap.

Nothing committed. `.entire/settings.json` is present, so a commit is clear to proceed whenever you want it.
Are there any of the images that are too small or borderline too small that would be candidates for upscaling?
Good question — "too small" splits into two different problems, and only one of them calls for upscaling. Let me get the actual numbers.

# Vision Pro angular resolution: ~3660x3200 per eye over ~100 deg H FOV -> ~34-37 px/deg.
PPD = 34.0
print(f"Assumed display limit: {PPD:.0f} px/deg (Vision Pro, ~3660px over ~100 deg FOV)\n")
print("EQUIRECT BACKDROPS — a 360-deg wrap, so px/deg = width/360")
print(f"{'asset':<30}{'shipped':>13}{'px/deg':>8}{'vs 34':>8}   {'source max':>13}  headroom")
rows = [
 ("deep_star_map_8k",      8192,  8192,  "NASA 16K/32K/64K published"),
 ("dual_nebula",           8192, 12000, "raw 12000"),
 ("blue_filaments",        8192, 15000, "raw 15000"),
 ("teal_orange",           8192, 12000, "raw 12000"),
 ("dark_dust",             8192, 12000, "raw 12000"),
 ("pale_haze",             8192, 12000, "raw 12000"),
 ("ground_rogland_night",  8192, 24576, "Poly Haven 24K"),
 ("ground_dikhololo_night",8192, 24576, "Poly Haven 24K"),
 ("ground_paranal_vlt",    8192, 26162, "raw 26162"),
 ("ground_paranal_lasers", 8192, 25786, "raw 25786"),
 ("ground_lasilla_arc",    8192, 23384, "raw 23384"),
 ("ground_lasilla_airglow",8192, 23692, "raw 23692"),
 ("imported_spatial_1",   12000, 12000, "shipped native"),
 ("imported_spatial_2",   15000, 15000, "shipped native"),
]
for name, ship, src, note in rows:

content.text:
Yes — one true upscale candidate, and a more consequential finding underneath the question.

## Only one image is genuinely too small

**`eso0932a`, the ESO Milky Way panorama — 6000×3000, or 0.49× of what the display resolves.** It's the only asset that would need actual upscaling, and I'd still advise against it. Upscaling a 2× deficit on astrophotography invents star detail rather than recovering it, and the asset is already superseded by Deep Star Maps at every resolution. Leave it as the look reference it's filed as.

**Nothing in the deep-sky set is close to too small.** They're L3 billboards, so what matters is angular size, not pixel count. The smallest master — Pillars infrared at 3249 px — supports a 96° object, and a §7c hero at 40–150 m subtends roughly 10–40°. It clears the worst case by 2.4×. No action needed on any of them.

## The finding worth acting on: every backdrop ships under display resolution

An equirect wraps 360°, so its angular resolution is just width ÷ 360. Vision Pro resolves ~34 px/deg (~3660 px over ~100° FOV):

| | shipped | px/deg | vs display |
|---|---|---|---|
| All 12 generated backdrops | 8192 | 22.8 | **0.67×** |
| `imported_spatial_1,3,4,5` | 12000 | 33.3 | 0.98× |
| `imported_spatial_2` | 15000 | 41.7 | 1.23× |

Matching the display needs ~12240 px wide. 8K lands a third short — and this isn't a fixable-by-upscaling problem, because **11 of the 12 have unused source headroom.** I downsampled 26K ESO panoramas and 24K Poly Haven HDRIs to 8K to match the existing convention. The pixels are sitting in `_raw/`.

The one exception is `deep_star_map_8k`, whose raw EXR is genuinely 8192 — but NASA publishes that same map at 16K, 32K and 64K, so it's a re-download rather than an upscale.

**This has a direct bearing on the A/B you've been running.** The imported Apple Spatial skies ship at 0.98–1.23× while the generated scene's backdrop sits at 0.67×. Some of what reads as "the imported image looks better" is a resolution gap, not a content or stereo gap. Worth knowing before drawing conclusions from that comparison.

The grounded panoramas benefit most from a bump. Softness is nearly invisible in a star field — it's mostly empty. It's very visible in a rocky desert floor that reads as a couple of metres below you, and that ground is exactly where the eye rests in a scene whose whole job is being a stable reference.

## What it costs

| Width | px/deg | Bundle (12 files) | Decoded RGBA8 +mips | ASTC 4×4 +mips |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8192 (now) | 22.8 | 48 MB | 179 MB | 45 MB |
| **12288** | **34.1** | **108 MB** | 402 MB | 100 MB |
| 16384 | 45.5 | 192 MB | 714 MB | 179 MB |

I'd go **12288×6144** — it matches the display almost exactly, costs 2.25× the pixels rather than 4×, and stays close to what `imported_spatial` already ships without issue. 16K overshoots what the optics resolve and triples memory for it.

Two caveats on that memory column. It assumes one resident backdrop, which is what `reskin` already does deliberately — that comment about six cached blowing the budget was about *caching*, not about one texture being too large. And §4 calls for ASTC, but these are currently loose HEIC loaded through `TextureResource(named:)`, so whether they land compressed on the GPU is something I'd want to confirm on device before committing to 12K. The ASTC column is a very different story from the RGBA8 one.

Say the word and I'll re-ingest all eleven at 12288 and pull Deep Star Maps at 16K — the tool takes a `-Z` target, so it's a parameter change, and the `busyness` values are resolution-independent so the catalogue won't need retuning.

A couple of thoughts. The pre-processed imported spatial images do better than every one of the normal images. I think we should shift to that as the standard. The generated nebulae to give depth just don't work, and they chew up resources that would be better used in rendering at the proper framerate. Same goes for the generated stars.

I've turned on my comfyui deployment and service on sdf1 to do the upscaling. There is a built config in the ~/Developer/Float/upscale folder
Two separate pieces of work there. Let me look at the upscale setup first since you've just brought the service up.

|---|---|---|---|
| ComfyUI | **0.24.0** (git HEAD 2026-06-09) | **v0.28.0** (2026-07-15) | 228 commits, 4 releases |
| Container image | `COMFYUI_NVIDIA_DOCKER_VERSION: 20251228` | — | ~7 months |

Worth knowing: the image tag is `ubuntu22_cuda12.4.1-latest` but `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent`, so the `-latest` suffix is doing nothing — the node keeps serving whatever it cached in December. And ComfyUI itself lives in the PVC (`/comfy/mnt/ComfyUI`), not the image, so updating them is two independent operations: `git pull` + requirements in the volume, and `imagePullPolicy: Always` + restart for the CUDA base.

**My advice is to hold off.** The GPU is a **GTX 1070, 8 GB** — Pascal, sm_61. It's on torch 2.6.0+cu124 and working; newer ComfyUI pulls newer torch, and Pascal support is being wound down upstream. There's no feature in 0.25–0.28 we need, so upgrading is pure downside risk on the one card you have.

## Three blockers in the workflow regardless of version

- **`UltimateSDUpscale` isn't installed.** `custom_nodes/` contains only `__pycache__` and the stock examples — no custom nodes at all, and no ComfyUI-Manager, so installing means manual clone + pip.
- **`realisticVisionV60B1_v51VAE.safetensors` isn't there.** Checkpoints has `DreamShaper_8_pruned` and `v1-5-pruned-emaonly`.
- **`4x_NMKD-Siax_200k.pth` isn't there.** `upscale_models/` is empty.

Also, `build_wf.py` says *"tiled_decode=True to survive 75MP VAE decode on 32GB."* It's 8 GB of VRAM. Tiling makes 75 MP survivable, but on a 1070 at 18 steps across ~90 tiles you're looking at a long grind per image, times twelve.

## The thing I'd flag hardest: upscaling is the wrong tool for these particular assets

The config comment says *"upscale_by=6.4 (1920w source -> ~12288)"*. A 1920-wide source is exactly what NASA SVS was serving — and that's what I replaced in the first pass with true originals at 6780–11472 px. Those inputs don't exist in the project any more.

For the backdrops, **11 of 12 have native source headroom already sitting in `_raw/`**: 23–26K from ESO, 24K from Poly Haven, 12–15K from Shutterstock. Getting to 12288 is a *downscale*, not an upscale. Running a diffusion upscaler over an 8K downsample when a 26K original is on disk would actively destroy real detail and replace it with invented detail. The twelfth, `deep_star_map_8k`, is a free re-download from NASA at 16K/32K/64K.

Three more reasons it's a poor fit for equirect specifically, independent of hardware:

- **The seam.** Chess tiling processes tiles independently with no wrap padding. An equirect's left and right edges must match exactly — `DepthLayers` even notes "a proper 2:1 equirect hides it." Diffusion tiling would put a visible seam down the back of the sphere.
- **The poles.** Zenith and nadir collapse to a point, and diffusion detail there smears into swirls. On the grounded panoramas the nadir is directly underfoot, which is where the eye rests.
- **Hallucination on astrophotography.** The positive prompt asks for "realistic photograph, natural texture, fine detail." An SD1.5-class model has no useful prior for star fields; it will invent stars and clump nebulosity. That's also awkward against the CC BY credit lines we're shipping — the attribution says ESO/Poly Haven took the photo.

If you want that GPU earning its keep on Float, the better job is the one your architecture shift actually creates: **mono → stereo depth generation**, turning the six new grounded panoramas into Apple Spatial-format stereo pairs. That's a depth-estimation problem the box is genuinely suited to, and it's the missing step between "we have 26K equirects" and "everything ships as pre-processed spatial."

## On the architecture change

I haven't started it — flagging scope before I cut. You've been in the headset and I haven't, and "does this read as depth" is exactly the on-device judgement `TICKETS.md` marks `agent:needs-human`, so I'm taking the call as settled. Dropping L2/L3 retires `NebulaVolume`, the star field, `nebulaPalette`/`nebulaDensity`/`starDensity`/`starColorMix` in `EnvironmentConfig`, most of `EnvironmentGenerator`'s sub-streams, and the `busyness` suppression logic I just wired — which is fine, that was always in service of the generated nebula.

The one thing I'd want your read on before cutting: the §7e determinism guarantee and saved Places exist to reproduce *generated* universes. If the content is a fixed library of spatial images, seeds mostly stop meaning anything and Places becomes "which image + orientation." That's a simplification, not a problem — but it's a spec-level change to §5/§7e, so I'd rather you confirm than have me quietly gut it.

Want me to do the L2/L3 removal now, or settle the spatial-conversion pipeline first so the two land together?
Confirmed on the determinism shift. Let's do the spatial-conversion pipeline first.
Right call — the format is the constraint everything else hangs off, so let me nail down exactly what the existing `imported_spatial` HEICs are before designing anything.
